title: 第 60 節
en: 12. Then in the same space (ether) he came IV KHANDA, 4. 151 towards a woman, highly adorned : it was Uma, the daughter of Himavat1. He said to her: 'Who is that sprite ? '
Fourth Kha^da.
1. She replied : ' It is Brahman. It is through the victory of Brahman that you have thus become great.' After that he knew that it was Brahman.
2. Therefore these Devas, viz. Agni, Vayu, and Indra, are, as it were, above the other gods, for they touched it (the Brahman) nearest 2.
3. And therefore Indra is, as it were, above the other gods, for he touched it nearest, he first knew it.

4. This is the teaching of Brahman, with regard to the gods (mythological) : It is that which now 1 Uma may here be taken as the wife of Siva, daughter of Himavat, better known by her earlier name, Parvati, the daughter of the mountains. Originally she was, not the daughter of the mountains or of the Himalaya, but the daughter of the cloud, just as Rudra was originally, not the lord of the mountains, girija, but the lord of the clouds. We are, however, moving here in a secondary period of Indian thought, in which we see, as among Semitic nations, the manifested powers, and particularly the knowledge and wisdom of the gods, represented by their wives. Uma means originally flax, from va, to weave, and the same word may have been an old name of wife, she who weaves (cf. duhit/7', spinster, and possibly wife itself, if O. H. G. wib is connected with O. H. G. weban). It is used almost synonymously with ambika, Taitt. Ar. p. 839. If we wished to take liberties, we might translate uma haimavati by an old woman coming from the Himavat mountains ; but I decline all responsibility for such an interpretation.

2 The next phrase was borrowed from § 3, without even changing the singular to the plural. As Indra only found out that it was Brahman, the original distinction between Indra and the other gods, who only came near to it, was quite justified. Still it might be better to adopt the var. lect. sa hy etat in § 2.
flashes forth in the lightning, and now vanishes again.

5. And this is the teaching of Brahman, with regard to the body (psychological) : It is that which seems to move as mind, and by it imagination remembers again and again \ 6. That Brahman is called Tadvana 2, by the name of Tadvana it is to be meditated on. All beings have a desire for him who knows this.

7. The Teacher : ' As you have asked me to tell you the Upanishad, the Upanishad has now 1 I have translated these paragraphs very differently from «Sahkara and other interpreters. The wording is extremely brief, and we can only guess the original intention of the Upanishad by a reference to other passages. Now the first teaching of Brahman, by means of a comparison with the gods or heavenly things in general, seems to be that Brahman is what shines forth suddenly like lightning. Sometimes the relation between the phenomenal world and Brahman is illustrated by the relation between bubbles and the sea, or lightning and the unseen heavenly light (Mait. Up. VI, 35). In another passage, Kh. Up. VIII, 12, 2, lightning, when no longer seen, is to facilitate the conception of the reality of things, as distinct from their perceptibility. I think, therefore, that the first simile, taken from the phenomenal world, was meant to show that Brahman is that which appears for a moment in the lightning, and then vanishes from our sight.

The next illustration is purely psychological. Brahman is proved to exist, because our mind moves towards things, because there is something in us which moves and perceives, and because there is something in us which holds our perceptions together (sahkalpa), and revives them again by memory.

1 give my translation as hypothetical only, for certainty is extremely difficult to attain, when we have to deal with these enigmatical sayings which, when they were first delivered, were necessarily accompanied by oral explanations.
2 Tadvana, as a name of Brahman, is explained by ' the desire of it,' and derived from van, to desire, the same as \inkh.
IV KllANDA, 9. 15 been told you. We have told you the Br ah ml Upanishad.
8. ' The feet on which that Upanishad stands are penance, restraint, sacrifice; the Vedas are all its limbs x, the True is its abode.
9. ' He who knows this Upanishad, and has shaken off all evil, stands in the endless, unconquerable2 world of heaven, yea, in the world of heaven.'

1 It is impossible to adopt Ankara's first rendering, ' the Vedas and all the Ahgas/ i.e. the six subsidiary doctrines. He sees himself that sarvahgani stands in opposition to pratish//;a and ayatana, but seeing Veda and Ahga together, no Brahman could help thinking of the Vedahgas.
2 Might we read a^yeye for ^yeye ? cf. -Satap. Brahm. XI, 5, 7, 1.
First Khanda.
i. Now follows the Mahavrata ceremony.
2. After having killed Wz'tra, Indra became great. When he became great, then there was the Mahavrata (the great work). This is why the Mahavrata ceremony is called Mahavrata.
3. Some people say : ' Let the priest make two (recitations with the offering of the) a^ya (ghee) on that day,' but the right thing is one *.
4. He who desires prosperity should use the hymn, pra vo devayagnaye (Rv. Ill, 13, 1).
5. He who desires increase should use the hymn, vi.ro vi.ro atithim (Rv. VIII, 74, 1).

1 That it should be one only is proved from the types, i.e. from other sacrifices, that have to be followed in the performance of the Mahavrata. The first type is the Agnish/oma, where one jastra is enjoined as ag-ya^astra, viz. pra vo devayagnaye. In the Vijvagit, which has to follow the Agnish/oma, another hymn is put in its place, viz. agni/ra naro didhitibhi//. In the Mahavrata, which has to follow the Vi^va^it, some people recommend the use of both these hymns. But that is wrong, for there must be in the sacrifices which follow the Agnish/oma twelve .rastras altogether ; and if there were two here, instead of one, we should get a total of thirteen.
6. The people (visa/i) indeed are increase 1, and therefore he (the sacrificer) becomes increased.

7. But (some say), there is the word atithim (in that hymn, which means a guest or stranger, asking for food). Let him not therefore take that hymn. Verily, the atithi (stranger) is able 2 to go begging.
8. ' No,' he said, ' let him take that hymn.
9. ' For he who follows the good road and obtains distinction, he is an atithi (guest) 3.
10. ' They do not consider him who is not so, worthy to be (called) an atithi (guest).
zh: 12. 其後在同一空間（乙太）中，他朝著一位盛裝打扮的女子走去：她是喜馬拉雅山之女烏瑪。他對她說：「那是誰？」
第四卡陀
她回答：「那是梵天（Brahman）。正因為梵天的勝利，你才因此變得偉大。」之後他才知道那是梵天。
2. 因此這些提婆，即阿耆尼（Agni）、伐由（Vayu）和因陀羅（Indra），在某種意義上是在其他諸神之上的，因為他們最接近地接觸到它（梵）²。
3. 因此，因陀羅在某種意義上高於眾神之上，因為他最接近地觸及了它，他首先認識了它。

4. 這是關於諸提婆（神話的）的梵天（Brahman）教說：它就是現在的，Uma 可以視為濕婆（Siva）的妻子，喜馬拉雅山神（Himavat）的女兒，更早時期以帕瓦蒂（Parvati）之名為人所知，即山岳之女。本來她不是山岳或喜馬拉雅的女兒，而是雲的女兒，就如同魯陀羅（Rudra）本來也不是山嶺之主（girija），而是雲之主。然而，我們在此處於印度思想的次生時期，在這個時期，我們看到如閃語族民族中那樣，諸神的顯現力量，特別是諸神的知識與智慧，通過其妻子而得到表現。Uma 本意為亞麻，源自 va（織），同一個詞可能曾是妻子的古老名稱，即織布者（參照 duhit/τ'，女紡織工，可能還有妻子本身，如果古高地德語 wib 與古高地德語 weban 相關聯的話）。它幾乎與 ambika 同義使用，見《泰氏梵書》（Taitt. Ar.）第 839 頁。倘若我們願意恣意詮釋，我們可以將 uma haimavati 譯為「來自喜馬拉雅山的老婦」；但我對這樣的詮釋不負任何責任。
下一句借自第三節，甚至連單數都未改為複數。因為因陀羅（Indra）唯獨發現這就是梵天（Brahman），所以因陀羅與其他諸神之間的原初區別是完全合理的——其他諸神僅只能接近它。儘管如此，採納第二節中的異文讀本 sa hy etat 可能會更佳。
在閃電中閃耀而出，轉瞬又消逝無蹤。
5. 梵天關於身體（心理學意義）的教導如下：即心靈般運動的那一切，想像藉由它而一再地回憶之物。6. 梵天被稱為「塔德瓦那」(Tadvana)，應當以塔德瓦那之名對其冥想。凡知曉此理者，一切眾生皆對其懷有渴望。

7. 教師曰：「既然汝已請我為汝述說奧義書，奧義書今已1我對這些段落的譯文與商羯羅及其他詮釋者大異其趣。其措辭極為簡潔，吾人唯有藉由參照其他段落，方能推測奧義書原始的意圖。今梵天（Brahman）的第一教導，藉由與諸神或一般天界事物的比較，似乎要說明梵天乃是如閃電般忽然閃耀而出的東西。有時，現象世界與梵天之間的關係，為了以泡沫與海、或閃電與不可見的天光（Mait. Up. VI, 35）之間的關係來說明。在另一段落中，《歌者奧義書》（Kh. Up.）VIII, 12, 2，閃電一旦不再被看見，便用來便於對事物實在性的認識，以區別於其可感知性。因此，我認為取自現象世界的第一個比喻，是為了要說明梵天乃是在閃電中顯現片刻、爾後從吾人視線中消失的東西。」
下一個例證純粹是心理學的。梵天（Brahman）的存在得以證明，因為我們的心靈趨向於萬物，因為我們體內有某物在運動並感知，而且因為我們體內有某物將我們的知覺聚合在一起（sahkalpa），並透過記憶再度喚醒它們。
我僅將我的翻譯作為假設性的，因為當我們必須處理這些謎一般的言說時，確定性極為難以達致，而這些言說在最初傳述時，勢必伴隨著口頭上的解釋。
2 特德瓦那（Tadvana）作為梵天的名稱，被解釋為「它的欲望」，源自梵文動詞 van（欲望），與希臘文 ἴγχ 同源。
第四，克羅達，9. 15 已向你述說。我們已向你述說了梵天奧義書。
8. 「那部奧義書所立足的基礎乃苦行、克制、祭祀；吠陀乃其各肢體，真理乃其所居。
9. 「凡知此奧義書者，已拋棄一切邪惡，立足於無盡、不可戰勝²的天界世界，是的，立足於天界世界。」
無法採用安卡拉（Ankara）的首次解讀「吠陀及所有阿格」（the Vedas and all the Ahgas），即六部附屬教義。他自己也看得出 sarvahgani 與 pratishtha 和 ayatana 相對立，但既然吠陀與阿格並列，任何婆羅門都不禁會想起吠陀阿格（Vedahgas）。
2 我們能否將 a^yeye 讀作 ^yeye？參見《百道梵書》（Satapatha Brahmaṇa）第十一卷第五章第七節第一段。
第一篇章
現在接著是大誓願儀式（Mahavrata）。
2. 因陀羅殺死弗栗多（Wz'tra）之後變得偉大。當他變得偉大之時，便有了摩訶婆多（Mahavrata，偉大的事業）。這就是為什麼摩訶婆多儀式被稱為摩訶婆多。
3. 有人說：「讓祭司在那一天以（獻祭牛酥的）誦經進行兩次（儀式），」但正確的做法是一次。*
4. 欲求昌隆者應當誦唱讚歌（pra vo devayagnaye），《梨俱吠陀》卷三，13，1。
5. 欲求增進者應當誦唱該讚歌：vi.ro vi.ro atithim（《梨俱吠陀》第八卷，74，1）。

其一為唯一的，乃由各類型證明之，即由其他祭祀證明之，此等祭祀必須在大祭（Mahavrata）之行禮中遵循。首類型為火酥瑪祭（Agnish/oma），其中一首頌文被規定為祭祀頌文（ag-ya^astra），即「願汝等趨向祭祀諸神」（pra vo devayagnaye）。在必須遵循火酥瑪祭之普遍祭祀（Vijvagit）中，另一首頌文代之，即「火神以光輝引導眾人」（agni/ra naro didhitibhi//）。在必須遵循普遍祭祀之大祭中，有人建議使用此二首頌文。但此乃錯誤之舉，因為在遵循火酥瑪祭之諸祭祀中，總共必須有十二首頌文；若此處有二首而非一首，吾人將得到十三首之總數。
6. 民眾（visa/i）實為增長，因此他（獻祭者）便得以增長。
7. 但有人說，那首讚歌中有「atithim」這個字（意為客人或陌生人，乞求食物）。因此他不應取用那首讚歌。誠然，atithi（陌生人）是能夠去乞討的。
「不，」他說，「讓他取那首讚歌吧。
9. '蓋凡循正道而獲殊榮者，彼乃為「阿提西」（atithi，客人）3。
10. 「他們不認為不是這樣的人值得被（稱為）атithі（客人）。」
